babyDevelopment: Fertilization happens as sperm meets egg
The fertilized egg (zygote) begins dividing
The blastocyst travels toward the uterus
momSymptoms: No symptoms yet — you may not even know you're pregnant
momTips: Start taking folic acid (400–800 mcg daily)
Avoid alcohol, smoking, and raw fish
Schedule a preconception appointment with your OB
checklistItems: Begin prenatal vitamins with folic acid
Stop drinking alcohol and smoking

babyDevelopment: The blastocyst implants into the uterine lining
Cells differentiate into embryo and placenta
The placenta begins producing hCG hormone
momSymptoms: Mild cramping or spotting from implantation
momTips: Continue folic acid supplementation
Avoid hot tubs and saunas
Stay hydrated — aim for 8–10 glasses of water daily

babyDevelopment: The neural tube (brain and spinal cord) starts developing
The heart begins to form
Three layers of cells differentiate (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm)
momTips: Take a home pregnancy test if your period is late
Call your OB to schedule your first prenatal visit
Avoid NSAIDs like ibuprofen — use acetaminophen instead
checklistItems: Take a pregnancy test
Call OB for first prenatal appointment

babyDevelopment: The embryo is the size of a poppy seed
The heart tube is forming and will soon begin beating
Arm and leg buds appear
momTips: Begin documenting pregnancy milestones
Eat small, frequent meals to manage nausea
Get plenty of rest — your body is working hard
checklistItems: Confirm pregnancy with OB blood test
Discuss any medications with your doctor

babyDevelopment: The tiny heart begins beating (~100 bpm)
Brain and spinal cord development accelerates
Eyes and ears start forming
momSymptoms: Strong morning sickness for many women
Light spotting can still be normal — call OB if heavy
momTips: Try ginger tea or crackers for nausea
Eat folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, citrus
Rest when you can — fatigue is intense in the first trimester
checklistItems: Schedule first prenatal ultrasound (usually at 8–10 weeks)
Start tracking symptoms in a journal

babyDevelopment: The heart beats 100–160 times per minute
Facial features begin forming (eyes, nostrils, mouth)
Tiny arm and leg buds are growing
momSymptoms: Nausea is often at its worst now
momTips: Eat bland, easy-to-digest foods when nauseous
Stay hydrated — dehydration worsens nausea
Talk to your partner about how you're feeling

babyDevelopment: The brain is growing at 100 new cells per minute
Eyelids are forming
Intestines are developing in the umbilical cord
momTips: Don't skip meals even if you feel sick
Try vitamin B6 for nausea relief (consult your doctor)
Light exercise like walking can boost your mood

keyMilestone: Officially an embryo → fetus transition begins
babyDevelopment: All major organs are forming
Fingers and toes are beginning to develop
Tail disappears as spine straightens
momTips: Wear a supportive bra as breasts grow
Start researching healthcare providers and birth plans
Avoid cat litter — toxoplasmosis risk
checklistItems: First prenatal appointment typically this week
Blood work: blood type, CBC, STI screening

babyDevelopment: Now officially called a fetus
Eyelids fuse shut (will reopen at 28 weeks)
Toes are visible and fingers are longer
momTips: Rest when tired — your body is doing incredible work
Start Kegel exercises for pelvic floor health
Stay away from deli meats and unpasteurized cheeses

babyDevelopment: External genitalia beginning to differentiate
Vital organs are fully formed and growing
Baby begins making small movements (too small to feel)
momSymptoms: Nausea may start to ease slightly
Visible belly bump for some women (especially second+ pregnancies)
momTips: Consider prenatal yoga or swimming
Discuss NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing) with your OB
Start moisturizing your belly to support skin elasticity
checklistItems: Discuss genetic testing options: NIPT, nuchal translucency scan

babyDevelopment: Baby may start hiccuping
Tooth buds forming beneath gum line
Fingers and toes fully separated
momSymptoms: Linea nigra (dark belly line) may appear
momTips: Use sunscreen daily — hormones make skin more sun-sensitive
Continue light exercise
Stay connected with other expecting parents

keyMilestone: End of first trimester — miscarriage risk drops significantly
babyDevelopment: Reflexes develop — baby can open and close fists
Digestive system is practicing contractions
All critical organs are formed
momSymptoms: Morning sickness often begins to ease
Energy levels may start to improve
momTips: Share your pregnancy news — miscarriage risk drops to ~2%
Start researching childbirth classes
Consider maternity clothes — your waistline is expanding
checklistItems: Nuchal translucency ultrasound (weeks 11–14)
Announce pregnancy to family and friends if desired

babyDevelopment: Unique fingerprints are forming on fingertips
Vocal cords developing
Baby can suck thumb
momSymptoms: Second trimester begins — often called the 'golden trimester'
Round ligament pain as uterus grows
momTips: Enjoy the energy boost of the second trimester
Start planning your nursery
Consider registering for a baby shower registry

babyDevelopment: Lanugo (fine hair) covers baby's body for warmth
Baby practices breathing by inhaling amniotic fluid
Kidneys producing urine
momTips: Begin sleeping on your side — left side is best for circulation
Start using a pregnancy pillow for comfort
Schedule your anatomy scan (typically at 18–20 weeks)
checklistItems: Schedule anatomy ultrasound for weeks 18–20

babyDevelopment: Bones are hardening with calcium deposits
Baby can sense light through eyelids
Taste buds developing on tongue
momSymptoms: Heartburn may begin as uterus pushes up
Nose bleeds and congestion from increased blood volume
momTips: Eat smaller, more frequent meals to combat heartburn
Sleep propped up on pillows to reduce acid reflux
Stay active with walking, swimming, or prenatal yoga

babyDevelopment: Baby is making coordinated movements
Eyes move (though still fused shut)
Ears are fully developed — baby can hear your voice
momSymptoms: Back pain may begin as posture shifts
Glowing skin for many thanks to increased blood flow
momTips: Talk and sing to your baby — they can hear you!
Start browsing baby names
Consider a prenatal massage for back pain relief
checklistItems: Optional: Quad screen blood test (weeks 15–20) for chromosomal screening

babyDevelopment: Baby accumulates fat for warmth and energy
Sweat glands developing
Umbilical cord grows stronger and thicker
momTips: Moisturize belly, hips, and thighs to help with stretch marks
Eat protein-rich foods to support baby's growth
Rest with legs elevated to reduce swelling

babyDevelopment: Baby is yawning, hiccuping, and rolling around
Myelin sheath begins coating nerves
Ears are in final position — hearing is well developed
momSymptoms: Most women feel movements clearly now
Possible dizziness from low blood pressure
momTips: Lie on your left side to prevent dizziness
Stretch legs before bed to prevent cramps
Stay well hydrated and take magnesium if cramps persist (ask your OB)
checklistItems: Anatomy ultrasound scan this week (18–20 weeks)
You may be able to find out the baby's sex at this scan

babyDevelopment: Vernix caseosa (waxy coating) forms on skin for protection
Sensory development accelerates — touch, taste, smell, hearing, sight
Baby has a regular sleep/wake cycle
momSymptoms: Vivid dreams are very common in pregnancy
momTips: Wear a belly support band for back and ligament pain
Do prenatal stretches daily
Start thinking about a birth plan

babyDevelopment: Halfway through pregnancy — major milestone!
Hair is growing on the scalp
Baby is swallowing amniotic fluid and practicing digestion
momSymptoms: Belly button may start to pop out
Possible varicose veins from increased blood volume
momTips: Take a 20-week bump photo to celebrate the milestone
Start practicing kick counts (typically begins at 28 weeks)
Register for childbirth education classes now — they fill up fast
checklistItems: Book your childbirth education class
Begin researching pediatricians

babyDevelopment: Baby is swallowing several ounces of amniotic fluid per day
Taste buds are fully developed
Bone marrow starts making blood cells
momSymptoms: Increased appetite — the 'eating for two' phase
Skin changes continue (stretch marks, darkening nipples)
momTips: Eat iron-rich foods: lean red meat, beans, spinach
Stay active — aim for 30 minutes of moderate exercise most days
Start your baby registry if you haven't already

babyDevelopment: Eyebrows and eyelashes are now visible
Baby looks like a newborn in miniature
Grip is strong enough to grasp the umbilical cord
momSymptoms: Back pain common as center of gravity shifts
Swollen feet and ankles in the evening
momTips: Wear comfortable, supportive shoes
Elevate feet when sitting to reduce swelling
Consider a pregnancy support belt

babyDevelopment: Baby reaches viability — survival possible with intensive care
Lungs developing air sacs (alveoli)
Sense of balance developing via inner ear
momSymptoms: Shortness of breath as uterus pushes up on diaphragm
Possible carpal tunnel symptoms from fluid retention
momTips: Avoid lying flat on your back — use pillows for support
Wrist splints can help with carpal tunnel
Discuss your birth plan ideas with your healthcare provider

babyDevelopment: Lungs start producing surfactant to help them expand after birth
Brain is growing rapidly
Baby responds to sound with increased heart rate or movement
momSymptoms: Gestational diabetes screening typically this week
momTips: Prepare for the glucose tolerance test (no sugary breakfast beforehand)
Do pelvic floor exercises to prevent hemorrhoids
Start touring hospital/birth center options
checklistItems: Glucose tolerance test (24–28 weeks)
Tour hospitals or birth centers

babyDevelopment: Baby is gaining fat rapidly for temperature regulation after birth
Nostrils begin to open
Baby responds to your touch through the belly
momSymptoms: Symphysis pubis dysfunction (pelvic girdle pain) possible
Increased urinary frequency as baby drops lower
momTips: Apply moisturizer or bio-oil to itchy belly skin
Use a pillow between knees when sleeping for pelvic relief
Start writing your birth plan

babyDevelopment: Eyelids open for the first time — baby can blink
Eyes are almost fully developed
Brain activity increases dramatically — baby is dreaming!
momSymptoms: Strong kicks and rolls — sometimes visible from outside
momTips: Create a relaxing bedtime routine to combat insomnia
Consider a pregnancy pillow for better sleep positioning
Start preparing the nursery

babyDevelopment: Last week of the second trimester
Brain tissue developing grooves and ridges
Baby is very active — you can feel stretches, kicks, and rolls
momSymptoms: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) common at night
momTips: Discuss Tdap vaccine (recommended at 27–36 weeks) with your OB
Begin counting daily kick counts
Research and select a pediatrician
checklistItems: Tdap vaccine (weeks 27–36)
Select a pediatrician

babyDevelopment: Third trimester begins — baby's survival rate is very high now
Eyes open and can distinguish light and dark
REM sleep established — baby is dreaming
momSymptoms: Back pain intensifies as baby grows
momTips: Begin formal kick counts — 10 movements in 2 hours is normal
Sleep on your left side — better for blood flow to baby
Prepare your hospital bag
checklistItems: Start formal kick count tracking
Enroll in infant CPR class
Begin preparing hospital bag

babyDevelopment: Brain is growing rapidly — developing more complex structures
Muscles and lungs continuing to mature
Baby is storing iron and calcium
momSymptoms: Frequent urination returns as baby presses on bladder
momTips: Sleep sitting up slightly to ease heartburn
Eat small meals more frequently
Pack your hospital bag this week

babyDevelopment: Fine lanugo hair falls off as baby gains fat
Eyes can focus and track light
Bone marrow now handles all red blood cell production
momSymptoms: Colostrum may begin leaking from breasts
momTips: Buy nursing pads for colostrum leaking
Discuss birth preferences with your care team
Take a tour of the labor and delivery unit
checklistItems: Pack hospital bag
Tour labor and delivery unit

babyDevelopment: Baby is gaining about half a pound per week now
All five senses are functional
Immune system boosted by antibodies from mom
momSymptoms: Shortness of breath most pronounced now
Leaky bladder when coughing, sneezing, or laughing
momTips: Do Kegel exercises to manage bladder leakage
Sleep with extra pillows for comfort
Finalize your birth plan and share it with your doctor

babyDevelopment: Fingernails have grown to the tips of the fingers
Toenails also forming fully
Baby is likely in head-down position now
momSymptoms: Braxton Hicks more frequent and stronger
momTips: Confirm baby's position with your OB
Practice your breathing and relaxation techniques
Stock your freezer with easy-to-reheat meals
checklistItems: Confirm baby's position (head-down?) at next appointment
Finalize birth plan

babyDevelopment: Skull bones remain soft and flexible for birth — all other bones hardening
Pupils can dilate and constrict in response to light
Baby is practicing breathing movements
momSymptoms: Difficulty walking due to pelvic pressure
momTips: Practice relaxation techniques for labor
Discuss pain management options with your doctor
Pre-register at the hospital to save time in labor
checklistItems: Pre-register at your hospital
Discuss pain management plan with OB or midwife

babyDevelopment: Lungs are almost fully mature
Central nervous system fully developed
Baby is gaining about an ounce per day
momSymptoms: Swelling (edema) in hands, feet, and face
Braxton Hicks may feel quite strong
momTips: Call your OB if swelling is sudden or severe — could indicate preeclampsia
Rest with feet elevated to manage edema
Review signs of labor with your birth team
checklistItems: Review signs of preterm labor — when to call the doctor

babyDevelopment: Baby is filling out — little fat under the skin now visible
Kidneys fully developed
Hearing is sharp — familiar voices are recognized
momTips: Get GBS (Group B Strep) test done (weeks 35–37)
Confirm your support team for labor
Walk daily to help with baby's positioning
checklistItems: GBS (Group B Strep) swab test (weeks 35–37)

keyMilestone: Baby considered early term at 37 weeks
babyDevelopment: Baby dropping lower into the pelvis (lightening)
Gums are ridge-like in preparation for sucking
Immune system boosting from mom's antibodies
momSymptoms: Easier to breathe as baby drops (lightening)
More bladder pressure after baby drops
momTips: Channel nesting energy into productive tasks — stock up on supplies
Keep your phone charged and bag packed
Know the difference between Braxton Hicks and real contractions
checklistItems: Finalize newborn care plans
Install and inspect car seat (have it checked at a fire station)

babyDevelopment: Baby is considered 'early term' — organs are nearly complete
Grasp reflex is very strong
Baby has about 15% body fat
momTips: Rest when you can — labor is close!
Know the signs of labor: regular contractions, water breaking, bloody show
Have your birth partner review the birth plan

babyDevelopment: Brain and nervous system continue fine-tuning
Meconium accumulating in intestines
Lanugo almost all gone — smooth baby skin!
momSymptoms: Cervix may be dilating and effacing at OB checks
Extreme fatigue mixed with bursts of energy
momTips: Call your OB or go to the hospital if: contractions 5 min apart for 1 hour, water breaks, heavy bleeding
Stay home as long as safely possible in early labor
Practice your coping strategies for contractions
checklistItems: Confirm all logistics: who drives to hospital, who watches pets/older children

babyDevelopment: Baby is full term — lungs, brain, and liver are ready for life outside
Baby continues to gain weight until birth
Most of the vernix caseosa has dissolved
momSymptoms: Pre-labor signs: loss of mucus plug, bloody show
Emotional mix of anticipation and anxiety
momTips: Walk to encourage baby's descent
Stay in close contact with your doctor or midwife
Rest as much as possible before labor begins

babyDevelopment: Baby is fully developed and ready to be born!
Average weight: 7–8 lbs, length: ~20 inches
Skull bones remain soft for birth canal passage
momSymptoms: Due date — but only 5% of babies arrive exactly on their due date
Regular contractions are the main sign of true labor
momTips: If you haven't gone into labor, discuss induction options with your OB
Call your OB immediately if: water breaks, heavy bleeding, baby stops moving
Trust your body — you've got this!
checklistItems: Post-due-date: discuss induction plan with OB if no labor by 41 weeks
